text: This video shows a total laparoscopic hysterectomy after open myomectomy on a huge polypneumatus
The surgeon begins the procedure by freeing the lateral attachments of the uterus, using surgical instruments to detach the uterus from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon uses bipolar forceps and scissors to progressively cut the round ligament and the proximal part of the fallopian tube, addressing the large size of the uterus.

The surgeon carefully controls the proximal part of the fibrous adnexal vein, which is visibly affected by the previous open procedure. Using precise techniques, they slowly cut through the coagulated tissue.
that allows you to work in a dry working area.
The surgeon applies localized bipolar energy to the fibrous tissue, aiming to enhance the coagulation effect and minimize the risk of tissue isolation by the surrounding peritoneal tissue.
The surgeon carefully uses a bipolar device to coagulate and cut only the previously coagulated tissue. Elective bipolar coagulation is applied to the vessel after it has been skeletized.
The surgeon treats the proximal part of the adnexal pedicle using bipolar coagulation, carefully managing the fibrous tissue to ensure effective hemostasis.
The surgeon continues dissection in the vesicovaginal area, carefully separating tissue layers in this region.
This patient had a previous laparotomic myomectomy and a two C-section. So it's normal to find the bladder with this adhesion on the cervical ismic area.
The assistant applies cranial traction to the bladder, aiding in the identification of the correct dissecting plane. The surgeon then removes fibrotic tissues following their coagulation.
The surgeon carefully pushes the bladder to increase the working space, ensuring it remains within the correct dissection plane to avoid any danger.

The surgeon coagulates the round ligament on the opposite side and cuts the coagulated tissue. The peritoneum is incised between the bladder and the right part of the uterus, enhancing the mobility of the uterus.
The surgeon frees the bladder completely, revealing the anterior valve of the uterine manipulator clearly in the surgical field.
It's a benign surgery, it's not a prolapse surgery, so the dissection must be as limited as possible.

The surgeon performs dissection on the opposite side, coagulating the right fallopian tube approximately one centimeter from the cornea. The right ovary is individualized, followed by coagulation of the utero-ovarian pedicle.
The surgeon completes the dissection, preparing to coagulate the separated tissue structures individually.
The surgeon coagulates the vessel and the posterior leaf of the broad ligament, applying energy to achieve perfect hemostasis.

The surgeon controls the entire right uterine pedicle, beginning with its coagulation. The pedicle is not cut until the uterine pedicle on the opposite side is controlled to minimize the risk of hemorrhage from the uterine vein.

The surgeon controls the left uterine pedicle, encountering significant fibrotic tissue surrounding it. Careful selective coagulation is performed to isolate the uterine pedicle from the fibrotic tissue.
The surgeon cuts the coagulated half of the tissue and begins to individualize the uterine artery. The peritoneum is coagulated separately to free the uterine artery from its surrounding tissue.

The surgeon achieves vascular control of the right pedicle and proceeds to cut the posterior leaf of the broad ligament. The upper part of the uterosacral ligament is visible, and energy is applied to the entire left uterine pedicle before cutting it safely.
The uterine artery is visible and has been effectively controlled using a bipolar electrosurgical device, ensuring hemostasis.

The surgeon controls the vena, located medial to the artery, and proceeds to manage the left pedicle, including the cervical-vaginal pedicle and the posterior pedicle, while addressing the upper part of the uterosacral ligament.
The surgeon controls the right uterine pedicle, which has been previously coagulated, and re-applies energy to safely cut through the large pedicle.
The surgeon coagulates the pedicle over a three-centimeter length before safely cutting it, demonstrating precise control and technique.

The surgeon reapplies energy to the top of the uterine artery before performing a colpotomy using a needle instead of a classic cam hook. The entire aspect of the vagina is cut using a uterine manipulator, starting with the lateral left part, followed by the upper part of the utero-sacral ligament. The same maneuver is repeated on the right side, cutting the right part of the vagina. Finally, the residual attachment of the vagina to the uterus is severed.

The surgeon uses a chardonnay knife to perform a hemisection on the large uterus, facilitating its extraction through the vaginal route. The uterus and cervix are divided into two pieces for removal, with caution taken to avoid damage to the bowel during the procedure.
The surgeon uses forceps to perform a vaginal extraction of the myoma, demonstrating the ease of removal through the vaginal approach.
The surgeon presents the excised uterine specimen, showing the removed tissue following the completion of the hysterectomy procedure.
The surgeon performs an inter-annexial hysterectomy, removing the fallopian tube to decrease the risk of ovarian cancer.
The surgeon applies bipolar energy to the tissue, ensuring it is far from the ovary, and prepares to repeat the maneuver on the opposite side.
The surgeon extracts the fallopian tube through a twelve millimeter trocar, which will subsequently be used for suturing the vaginal incision.
The surgeon uses a Vicryl Zero suture to begin closing the lateral part of the vaginal cuff.

The surgeon closes the posterior aspect of the vagina by suturing the upper part of the uterosacral ligament using a rudder knot, an extracorporeal knotting technique. The procedure is repeated on the left edge of the vagina, ensuring the vaginal mucosa is included in the stitch to minimize injury risk during specimen extraction.

The surgeon completes the closure of the vagina using a broader knot technique, ensuring the vaginal mucosa is included within the stitches to prevent post-operative complications. The closure is reinforced with additional knots depending on the vaginal length, resulting in a fully closed vaginal incision.
I hope that this video was interesting for our gynecologist colleague. Thank you for your attention.
